taxonomy: Haplopappus fuliginosus is a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. It is native to South America and belongs to the genus Haplopappus, which is a group of plants characterized by their daisy-like flowers and hairy leaves. The family Asteraceae is one of the largest and most diverse families of flowering plants, comprising over 23,000 species. Haplopappus fuliginosus is often classified as a type of thistle or a wildflower due to its distinctive appearance. It is a perennial plant that grows in a variety of habitats, from grasslands to mountainous regions.
uses: Haplopappus fuliginosus, also known as the Sooty Daisy, has been traditionally used in folk medicine for various purposes. Its roots and leaves have been employed to treat fever, rheumatism, and other ailments due to their purported anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. Some indigenous communities have also utilized the plant to create remedies for digestive issues, such as stomach pains and diarrhea.
